column3: Yama: God of death, judge of souls, ruler of Naraka (underworld).
column4: No Satan/devil; evil seen as impersonal or ascribed to gods’ wrath.

column3: Yima: First king, paradise ruler.
column4: Ahriman (Angra Mainyu): Principle of cosmic evil, adversary to Ahura Mazda; early dualism.

column3: No Yama figure. Yahweh rules a pantheon; disasters attributed to Yahweh or lesser gods.
column4: No Satan as Yahweh’s opponent; angels unnamed, disasters from Yahweh or lesser gods.
Monolatric reforms (Hezekiah, Josiah); polytheism persists, no devil figure.
Collapse of pantheon; shift to monotheism. Yahweh responsible for all; suffering prompts theological crisis.

Satan emerges as a heavenly being (Zechariah, Job); not yet God’s opponent, but a member of divine court. Dualistic thinking rises, influenced by Zoroastrianism. Names: Satan, Belial, Lucifer, Mastema, Azazel.
Satan/Devil becomes a personified evil; stories of fallen angels (e.g., Azazel, 1 Enoch); cosmic dualism.

column3: Yama as judge of the dead; Yamantaka (“Yama-slayer”) emerges, conquering Yama.
Yama becomes Yanluo Wang (China), Enma (Japan), ruler of the underworld.

column4: Satan/Devil becomes central adversary in Christian theology; explains evil, temptation, suffering. “Christianity cannot do without this character.”

column3: Yamantaka (Vajrabhairava): Wrathful, buffalo-headed, multi-armed; subdues Yama. Many forms (Krishna/Rakta Yamari).

column4: Devil/Satan becomes entrenched in European life and theology. St. Christopher, sometimes depicted as a monstrous or dog-headed figure, echoes underworld guardian motifs.

column3: Yemo / Yama & Variants Development
column4: Devil / Satan / Ahriman Development

column3: Yemo: Primordial twin; slain by Manu, his body becomes the world.
column4: No devil or adversary concept.
column5: Foundational twin myth of creation; Yemo’s death forms the cosmos.

column3:
Yama (Vedic India): First mortal, king of the dead.
Yima (Avestan Iran): First king, ruler of paradise.
column4: No personified evil; dualism not yet developed.
column5: Yemo splits into Yama and Yima, both rulers of the dead or paradise.

column3: Yama: God of death and justice, ruler of Naraka (underworld), judge of souls.
column4: No Satan/devil; evil seen as impersonal or ascribed to gods’ wrath.
column5: Yama depicted riding a buffalo, carrying noose/mace; judge of souls.

column3: Yima: First king, ruler of paradise.
column4: Ahriman (Angra Mainyu): Cosmic adversary, principle of evil opposing Ahura Mazda; early dualism.
column5: Ahriman embodies cosmic evil and destruction; dualistic cosmology emerges.

column3: No Yama figure. Yahweh rules a pantheon; disasters attributed to Yahweh or lesser gods.
column4: No Satan as Yahweh’s opponent; angels unnamed, disasters from Yahweh or lesser gods.
column5: Israelite religion was polytheistic; no concept of Satan as adversary.

column4: Monolatric/henotheistic reforms under Hezekiah and Josiah; polytheistic practice persists; no devil figure yet.
column5: Move toward worship of Yahweh alone, but polytheism persisted.

column4: Collapse of polytheistic pantheon; shift toward monotheism. Yahweh responsible for all events; theological crisis over suffering.
column5: Trauma from exile prompts rethinking of divine justice and suffering.

column4: Satan emerges as a heavenly being (Zechariah 3:1-2; Job 1:6-12; 1 Chronicles 21:1). Not yet Yahweh’s opponent; member of divine court. Increased angelology and dualistic thinking under Hellenistic influence.
column5: Satan, Belial, Lucifer, Mastema, Azazel appear as agents explaining suffering and evil.
column6: Biblical citations; scholarly consensus on Satan’s evolving role

column4: Satan/Devil personified as cosmic evil; stories of fallen angels (e.g., Azazel, 1 Enoch).
column5: Cosmic dualism and adversarial figures develop in apocalyptic literature.

column3:
Yama: Judge of the dead.
Yamantaka (“Yama-slayer”): Wrathful deity who conquers Yama, symbolizing victory over death.
column5: Yamantaka (Vajrabhairava) depicted with buffalo head, multi-armed; many forms including Krishna Yamari, Rakta Yamari.

column3: Yanluo Wang (Yama): Ruler of the underworld in Chinese Buddhism and Daoism.
column5: Adaptation of Yama as judge of the dead in East Asian traditions.

column3: Enma (Yama): Ruler of the afterlife, judge of souls.
column5: Japanese adaptation of Yama, often associated with Jizō, protector of souls.

column4: Satan/Devil becomes central adversary in Christian theology; explains evil, temptation, suffering. “Christianity cannot do without this character.”
column5: Devil becomes personified ultimate evil and tempter.

column3: Yamantaka (Vajrabhairava): Wrathful, buffalo-headed, multi-armed; subdues Yama. Many forms (Krishna Yamari, Rakta Yamari).
column5: Yamantaka embodies conquest over death and ignorance; wrathful protector deity.

column4:
Devil/Satan entrenched in European theology and culture.
St. Christopher: Sometimes depicted as dog-headed (cynocephalus), linked to underworld guardianship and psychopomp roles, echoing Yama-like motifs.
column5: St. Christopher’s monstrous depictions resonate with threshold guardian archetypes found in Yama and Anubis myths.
